matches.moment: A worked set-piece routine — Argentina's signature weapon all tournament — breaks a tense stalemate before England's late push falls just short.

verdict: The tournament's marquee rematch of 1986, 1998 and 2002 comes down to fine margins that the data says tilt, barely, toward Argentina. Both sides arrive fully rested off identical four-day turnarounds from extra-time quarterfinal wins, so fatigue is a wash and this is a straight quality contest. Argentina's case rests on three pillars: a 2.3 xG per game tournament average against just 0.7 xG conceded, five set-piece goals (the tournament high), and a Messi who is outscoring his own already-superb expected-goals tally (8 goals from 4.51 xG). England's case rests on Kane and Bellingham directly producing 12 of the team's 13 goals, a knockout run defined by resilience in two extra-time victories, and a marginal betting-market edge (37% to Argentina's 33%). The opponent-strength adjustment favors Argentina: their two toughest opponents to date, Egypt and Switzerland, were both beaten 3-goal hauls, showing their attacking numbers hold up against real quality rather than being inflated by group-stage minnows. England's own defensive line, by contrast, has needed extra time twice to close out games it should have won in regulation. That gap, plus Argentina's set-piece specialism against a defense that has not faced a team of comparable dead-ball threat, is enough to lean Argentina 2-1 — with the explicit caveat that a 1-1 draw is the strongest alternative and this is a Medium, not High, confidence call in a genuine coin-flip fixture. — The Analyst.

players.name: Lionel Messi
players.stat: 8 goals from 4.51 xG, 29 shots (17 on target), 27.59% conversion — tournament top scorer
players.watch: Overperforming his own elite xG; has scored against every knockout-stage opponent faced so far

players.name: Harry Kane
players.stat: 6 goals in 5 tournament appearances, part of a duo (with Bellingham) responsible for 12 of England's 13 goals
players.watch: Movement in the box against a Romero-Martinez pairing that has conceded in three straight knockout matches

players.name: Jude Bellingham
players.stat: 6 goals in 5 tournament appearances, including a match-winning double in the Norway quarterfinal
players.watch: Late-arriving runs into the box — the primary source of England's goals beyond Kane

players.name: Julian Alvarez
players.stat: Starting alongside Messi in a front two; fallback stat (goals/assists this tournament) — role: chance creation and secondary scoring threat
players.watch: Link-up combinations with Messi that pulled Egypt's and Switzerland's back lines out of shape
